policy_statement: Chicago will enable building design for disassembly and reuse by 2035.
mechanism_description: Designing buildings for future disassembly enables material reuse, which reduces landfill disposal and avoids emissions from manufacturing new construction materials.
typology_evidence_quote: When materials are reused, they are kept out of landfills and new material manufacturing is avoided.
primary_causal_pathway: Building design for disassembly and reuse → ↑ material recovery and reuse + ↓ landfill disposal + ↓ virgin material production → ↓ lifecycle GHG emissions → ↓ atmospheric forcing
causal_mechanism_detail: (1) The policy changes building design, construction standards, and end-of-life management so structures and components can be dismantled, sorted, and reused instead of being crushed and disposed. (2) The mediating physical process is that reusable materials displace demand for newly manufactured steel, cement, timber products, fixtures, and other construction inputs, while also reducing waste sent to landfills and associated disposal emissions. (3) The climate-relevant outcome is lower embodied emissions across the building lifecycle, with possible additional reductions in landfill methane or waste management emissions depending on the material stream.
dominant_pathway_test: The single biggest climate-relevant effect of policies using this mechanism is reduced lifecycle greenhouse gas emissions by enabling reuse of building materials and thereby avoiding production of new carbon-intensive materials and some landfill-related emissions.
mechanism_classification_reasoning:
1. Dominant causal pathway: the mechanism primarily reduces emissions by keeping construction materials in circulation and avoiding production of new materials, which is a direct mitigation pathway.  
2. Why this primary over alternatives: although the mechanism strongly advances circularity and resource productivity, Resource Efficiency describes the means, while the principal climate outcome is reduced greenhouse gas emissions; per the dominant-pathway rule, Mitigation is primary. It is not Adaptation because the mechanism does not mainly reduce hazard exposure, resilience risk, or climate damages. It is not Nature-Based Solutions because no ecosystem process is central to the effect.  
3. Edge cases applied: some instances may emphasize landfill avoidance or waste management more than embodied carbon, and some reused materials may have variable net emissions depending on transport, processing, and substitution rates. Even so, across policies using this mechanism, the dominant climate-relevant effect remains lifecycle emissions reduction from avoided virgin material production and waste disposal.  
4. Confirming applicability to all instances sharing this mechanism: any genuine policy under this mechanism requires design for future disassembly with the intent of reuse, which consistently operates through material recovery, reduced disposal, and displaced new material production; therefore the mitigation classification holds across instances.  
5. Secondary subtype assignment is deferred to policy-level evidence: whether a specific policy should later be tagged more specifically as circular economy, construction waste diversion, embodied carbon reduction, or deconstruction depends on the exact policy design and evidence in the individual policy text.
mechanism_edge_case_notes: Edge case: this mechanism could be mistaken for Resource Efficiency because it improves material productivity and circularity, but under the dominant-effect rule the primary classification is Mitigation since the main climate outcome is reduced embodied and waste-related emissions. In cases where landfill avoidance dominates because materials are largely non-methane-generating inert debris, the mitigation benefit still mainly comes from avoided virgin production rather than disposal emissions.
key_indicators: “enable”; “building design for disassembly and reuse”; “by 2035”; “materials are reused”; “kept out of landfills”; “new material manufacturing is avoided”
co_benefits: Waste reduction, landfill diversion, resource conservation, and potentially reduced local pollution from material manufacturing.
instance_edge_case_notes: Could be interpreted as target-setting because of “by 2035,” but the operative verb is “enable,” not a quantified target or binding mandate. It could also later be implemented through regulatory or procurement tools, but the statement itself is broader and strategic rather than instrument-specific.
